name: Cashew
crop_cycle_note: Perennial tree; first bearing in 3-4 years, economically productive for 25-30 years

growth_stages.name: Nursery / Seedling
characteristics: Grafted or seedling plant in polybag, height under 50 cm
care_focus: Partial shade and regular watering in nursery
Protect from damping off in overwatered beds

growth_stages.name: Vegetative Growth
characteristics: Rapid canopy expansion, multiple branch flushes
care_focus: Formative pruning to shape canopy and encourage branching
Regular basin irrigation in dry months for establishment

growth_stages.name: Flowering
characteristics: Terminal panicles with mixed male and hermaphrodite flowers
Panicle color ranges from yellow-green to pinkish depending on variety
care_focus: Avoid pesticide spray during peak flowering to protect pollinators
Monitor closely for tea mosquito bug, the most damaging pest at this stage

growth_stages.name: Fruit / Nut Development
characteristics: Cashew apple (pseudo-fruit) swelling with true nut attached at the base
care_focus: Sustain irrigation if dry spell occurs during fruit swell
Watch for anthracnose on developing nuts and apples in humid weather

growth_stages.name: Maturity / Harvest
characteristics: Cashew apple fully colored (yellow/red), nut hangs below and rattles when shaken
Fruits drop naturally at full maturity
care_focus: Collect fallen nuts daily to prevent quality loss and rot
Sun-dry nuts to proper moisture level before storage

operations: Flowering and early fruit set period - critical monitoring window
Avoid spraying insecticide during peak bloom hours to protect pollinators
Apply potash-rich fertilizer split to support flowering
precautions: Highest risk period for tea mosquito bug damage on flush and panicles
Cold, dewy mornings can favor powdery mildew on panicles

operations: Main harvest season - daily collection of fallen nuts
Sun-drying and proper storage of harvested nuts
precautions: Delayed collection in hot weather increases nut quality loss and mold
Watch for stem/root borer entry points on pruned surfaces

operations: Apply first fertilizer dose after soil moisture is adequate
Deweeding and basin management around tree base
Undertake canopy management pruning for airflow
precautions: Root/collar rot risk rises in poorly drained, waterlogged basins
Anthracnose can affect new vegetative flush in persistent humidity

operations: Prepare canopy (light pruning) ahead of flowering season
precautions: Monitor new flush for early tea mosquito bug activity before flowering starts

diseases.name: Anthracnose
symptoms: Irregular dark brown to black lesions on young leaves, twigs, panicles, and developing nuts/apples
Affected panicles may dry up (blossom blight), reducing fruit set
Infected nuts often shrivel or drop prematurely
treatment: Spray a copper-based fungicide (e.g. 1% Bordeaux mixture) at first sign of infection
Remove and destroy severely affected panicles and twigs
prevention: Prophylactic copper spray at flush and pre-flowering stages in humid regions
Prune to maintain airflow and reduce canopy humidity

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
symptoms: White powdery fungal coating on panicles, flowers, and young nuts
Flower and nut drop resulting in significantly reduced yield
Favored by cool, humid nights during flowering
treatment: Dust wettable sulphur on panicles at first sign, ideally in early morning
Repeat spray/dusting at 10-15 day intervals through the flowering period if humidity persists
prevention: Time sulphur application to coincide with flowering onset in disease-prone areas
Avoid excess shade/humidity buildup around the canopy

diseases.name: Die-Back
diseases.causal_agent: Fungal complex, often secondary to borer/pruning wounds
symptoms: Progressive drying of twigs and branches starting from the tip inward
Often follows borer infestation or unhealed pruning cuts
treatment: Prune well below the affected (dead) portion into healthy wood
Apply Bordeaux paste to all cut surfaces after pruning
prevention: Control stem borer promptly since wounds are common entry points

diseases.name: Damping Off (Nursery)
symptoms: Sudden collapse and wilting of young nursery seedlings
Water-soaked rotting visible at the collar/root region
treatment: Drench nursery beds with a recommended fungicide solution
Remove and destroy affected seedlings promptly to limit spread
prevention: Avoid overwatering and ensure good nursery bed drainage
Use sterilized potting mix for new seedlings

diseases.name: Leaf Blight
symptoms: Irregular brown patches on leaves, often starting from margins or tips
Premature leaf drop in severe, prolonged infection
treatment: Spray mancozeb or a recommended fungicide on affected foliage
Improve plant nutrition to support recovery
prevention: Avoid water stress and maintain balanced fertilization
Prune overcrowded canopy for better airflow

pests.name: Tea Mosquito Bug
symptoms: Dark brown to black necrotic spots on tender shoots, leaves, and panicles
Panicle drying and blossom blight in severe infestation
Considered the single most damaging cashew pest
management: Spray a recommended insecticide at new flush and early panicle emergence
Repeat spray after flowering if infestation persists, timed to avoid pollinator activity
Field sanitation to remove alternate weed hosts nearby

pests.name: Stem and Root Borer
symptoms: Gum exudation with bore holes at the trunk base and root collar
Frass (chewed wood fragments) visible near the entry hole
Progressive branch dieback and tree decline if untreated
management: Swab trunk base with a recommended insecticide paste periodically
Extract and destroy grubs by probing bore holes with a wire
Avoid trunk injuries and treat wounds/pruning cuts promptly

pests.name: Cashew Shoot Tip Caterpillar
symptoms: Webbed, chewed young shoots and leaves at the growing tip
Stunted new growth in heavily infested shoots
management: Prune and destroy webbed shoot tips
Spray a recommended insecticide on new flush if infestation is widespread

pests.name: Thrips
symptoms: Silvery streaks and scarring on the leaf undersurface
Leaf curling and bronzing in heavy infestation
management: Spray neem oil (2%) on affected foliage
Ensure adequate irrigation to reduce plant stress during hot weather

risk_conditions.impact: Sharp rise in powdery mildew risk during flowering
Flower/panicle drop and reduced fruit set, higher anthracnose risk
Nut/apple drop under moisture stress, higher thrips activity
Panicle and young fruit drop from physical damage
